So everybody's doing well? Yeah, they do. Good. Glad to hear it. One of the things that you will always hear us say at the agency, whether it's the Dixie and Agency, celebrity press, polishing, celebrity sites, whichever company it is, is that we fully believe that people buy people. They don't buy your products or services. They buy you as a person. And once they get to know you, like you, trust you, then they do business with you. So I don't care what part of our business you're in, you're going to hear us say that. And that is always a fundamental philosophy that we take, whether it's online, offline, in the books, whatever. That's why we offer those custom covers. So I always like to start out a little bit about myself, because most of the time people don't know my background. So the first thing I always start off with is that is my father. He is not anything else, although we have been questioned many, many times. And if you were one of those people that before dad made the introduction last night to my mother, thought that I might be something else, you are not alone. I remember being at a TJ Roll Letter Copywriting class when I was 18. I think I just turned 18. And TJ walks up to me and he's like, hi, you are? And I'm like, I'm Lindsay. And he's still shaking my hand. And he's like, and this is your father? And he goes, oh, OK, good. I was just making sure. So over and over again, dad is dad, not daddy, not anything else. He is my father, blood. So normally when I say that, the next thing I get is they're like, oh, well, Nick, Nick must be your husband. And no, Nick is great. I've known Nick since I was 14. But he has a wonderful wife, and it is not me. I put up with him on a daily basis in the office. I think that's enough. The next question that I always get when we brought Greg on to be our other partner about three years ago is, oh, well, Greg's your partner. Or Greg's your husband. No, Greg is not my husband either. He too has a wife and one little boy and another one on the way. But I'm not her. However, I did get married. We're coming up on two years in July. And most of the time, he's usually here. But he had to work late last night. But he does exist. So this is my husband, completely outside of the business. His name is Eric. And he is in commercial real estate. So it's nice that he understands the business mindset, because I don't think I could ever be with somebody who didn't understand being an entrepreneur and being a business owner. But he is not in my business, which is a fabulous thing, because we're both super competitive. And competing in a personal life is probably not the best idea you've ever had for a marriage. So that is my husband. If you guys are staying tomorrow for the wrap-up party, we'll get to meet him. He'll be out. So he does live, breathe. And it is not any of my three partners. We don't have any kids yet. But these are my two nieces. And I'm very, very blessed that they live about 20 minutes away from me. So we still do family dinner nights every Wednesday. My parents, me, my husband, my sister, her husband, her two kids, and my aunt. So we do those every Wednesday, so I get to see them a lot. Sydney's the youngest. She's two, and Kendall's the oldest. And she'll be five coming up here in March. You want to talk about children? These are my kids. That's Cooper. He is a half shih tzu, half Pomeranian. He is the sweetest, most loving dog. He is also the most stupid dog you'll ever meet in your entire life. He's kind of like Zha Zha Bing from Star Wars, like super excited and loving about everything. That's Cooper. My other one is Marley. And she is probably too smart for her own good. She's super manipulative, but also very loving. And then the last one is going to be Lucy. Good old sweet Lucy. Lucy, my husband and I used to always say, we're not really cat people. I always had dogs growing up. He had dogs, but he hated cats. And I didn't hate cats. I like cats, but I just never was really a cat person. And one day I get a text from my husband, who was going out for a run. And he's like, so there are two kittens next door. Just thought you should know. Honey, did you go and get the kittens? Like, what do you mean? And so he turned back around and went and saw the kittens. And so we adopted, we took in both the kittens and actually mother at that time. And Lucy found her way into my husband's heart. And this is his cat. And he would do anything for this cat. The other two we got homes for. But Lucy did make her way into a permanent member of our family. So that is my family and completely outside of my business life. I went to school at the University of Florida. I studied marketing, which sounds really, really great, because I am one of the few people that can actually say that they are now doing business and something that they graduated with. However, I learned absolutely nothing. I'll be the first one to tell you that. I learned time management. I learned how to have really good time in college and get up and pass the next day and graduate with honors. But I was not the person that wanted to go to school. My parents will tell you that every six months, they had to convince me to stay in school. I graduated with honors, so I did well. But I always wanted to get out and own my own business. That's what I wanted to do. And so every six months, including the six months before I graduated, I would have the exact same conversation with my mother. I'm coming home. I'm going to start my own business. No, you are not. You were staying in school and you're going to graduate. And we went through this literally every six months. But I did eventually graduate. And I got out of school. And I didn't know what I was going to do, like most people when they graduate college. What are you going to do next? So I followed my father's one piece of bad advice. And I went into real estate. And at that point in time, it was 2005. So the bubble was just bursting. So I think I had like three months of good real estate. And then it burst. And although it was a bad decision income wise, because it didn't produce any, it was a wonderful decision because I learned firsthand personal branding. And what it means to that people buy you, not the other realtor down the street, because I was selling the exact same product that somebody else was. So the reason people would buy for me is because of me. Or not buy for me is because of me. So I learned personal branding. Got out of real estate, was in it for about a year, and then got out. And at that point in time, Nick and dad had decided that they wanted to start another law practice. And as my mother will tell you, dad does this every couple of years. He decides it's going to be a great idea to start another law practice. He's in it for a couple of years, and then decides it's a great idea to get out. And then he does it again. So this was on the upcycle again that he was going to be the 30 again. And so Nick wanted to be Orlando Music Attorney. That's really, really, really what he wanted to be called. So at that point in time, they needed somebody to run a website. And they needed somebody to do online marketing. And Lindsey, you're young. You went to school for marketing. You were on Facebook the first two months. It was active because University of Florida was one of the first 13 colleges that it came to. Here you go. It sounds like a great job for you. So I dove right into it. I was very fortunate to have some wonderful mentors in social media and search engine optimization, one of which ran Barack Obama's both of his campaigns for social media. So I learned from the best. And I started optimizing everything for Orlando Music Attorney because that's what Nick wanted to be known as. And I did it. And he ranked. And no traffic came in. So the piece of that that I forgot is that I forgot to do my homework and figure out if people were actually searching for Orlando Music Attorney. They weren't. Everybody was searching for Orlando Music Lawyer. And so just that little switch in the synonym of the key phrases that we were optimizing for, great, we optimized. Is it great? Your website ranked. No traffic came in because nobody was searching for it. So that was really my first hands-on experience with search engine optimization, that step one is know your key words. Step one is to make sure that what you're calling your business, what you are promoting, people are looking for. So I started doing stuff for them more and more. And people started asking me locally, can you do this for me? Can you do this for me? And I kept saying no. And then all of a sudden one day I woke up and said, well, Lindsay, that's the business that you've always wanted to be in. What are you doing? So I started saying yes. And that's how I started getting clients. So that was a little over seven years ago that I started celebrity sites. It was about at the same time that we were really starting the celebrity burning agency. So they kind of were roughly alongside of each other. So that's a little bit about me. So let's jump right in to what we're going to cover today. We're going to talk about two things. First one's going to be about getting your house in order. And that's what I talk about as getting your website where it needs to be. You can spend all the time, money, and energy driving people to your website. But if your website doesn't do its job to capture its leads, then you've just wasted all that time, money, and energy. So sometimes people focus on the social media and the search engine optimization because those are the buzzwords. But they really need to be focusing on their website first. And once their website is there, then let's spend the money to do the other. So we're going to talk about 10 things that you need to have to make sure that your website is a marketing website. And that's a tool that we use because there is a difference in websites on if it's just a pretty static brochure website. Or if it's actually a marketing website. Is it marketing you? Does it have calls to action and all that? So we'll go through those 10 things. The second thing we're going to talk about is building your digital DNA. And that's really your digital footprint. So when somebody Googles you or Googles your company, what does your digital DNA say about you? Is it just your website? Is it nothing at all? Is it maybe something bad? What does your digital DNA say? So it's how to get your digital DNA to say what you want it to say, to have that third party credibility of who you are and what you do. There we go. OK, so 10 tips to make yourself a marketing website and become a living legend. The first one's going to be personality. So again, people buy people. They don't buy your products or services. They buy you as a person. Once they get to know you like you trust you, then they do business with you. So one example here is just simply putting your photo out in front of your website. So this is Brian Spicer. He's a financial planner. And that's a very simple example. You can obviously also do it with video. This is Jeff Serino. He's also a financial planner. And we do it with both photo and video here. But don't forget about your text on your website as well. So your blogging and your articles and the various things that you do written-wise, make sure that also shows your personality. Because remember, people see things visually. People hear things auditorially. And then they also read them. So maybe for whatever reason, they're not picking up on who you are because of all the pretty graphics and logos and all that. Make sure that it's also written. When I was getting married a couple years ago, if you followed me in my weekly alert, everything I talked about was my wedding. I always brought it back to a business-wise. So I was talking about my vendors and what they did or didn't do or their customer service. And so I was tying it all in. So it made sense. I wasn't just talking about shoes, just to talk about shoes. But that was my personality. And so people got to know me and who I was about. That's why I talk about my family in the beginning things. That's why Nick shows his boys. Because you can't not fall in love with his kids. So personality has to be on your website. Two is going to be credibility. And that's one of the reasons you guys are all here today, is to make sure that you take that mass media credibility and be able to insert it into your local marketing that you're doing. So you'll be able to say, as seen on ABC, NBC, CBS, and Fox. But if you don't have those logos, think of the things outside of this that you can put in. So are you part of the Better Business Bureau? Are you part of trade publications that are big in your specific niche? Because remember, you're not trying to get the masses. You're trying to get the people that want you, want to do business with you. So is there some sort of trade journal that you can also use? Nick also talked to it a little bit about it earlier. We do soon to be appearing on. So you guys now can immediately go home and change your website to say soon to be appearing on ABC, NBC, CBS, and Fox, because it's very, very true. On Christine's, I flipped through it too quickly, but one of the things that I like on Christine's website is that we always say put a caption with your photo. Because especially if it's a professionally done photo, people don't know if it's just stock imagery and you're just a really good looking person, or if you're actually the person that they're gonna do business with. So make sure you put your name with the photo anywhere on your website, anywhere on your marketing. And then also on Christine's, we put bestselling author and president, I think, Christine Ray. So again, we took that credibility and we wrote it in case that's where their eye went was to the photo, and then they read the caption versus going to the logos. So don't just put bestselling author or the forthcoming book or whatever it is in your bio. Make sure you're also putting it in other places. Three is going to be targeted metadata. And really it's targeted metadata and knowing your keywords. So don't do my mistake. Make sure you know your keywords and make sure that you use them in everything that you do online. If you don't know your keywords, go to Google and Google Keywords, Google's keyword tool. It's called the Keyword Planner, I think now, and it's part of AdWords. But it's a wonderful free tool that you can search for what you think people are searching for and it'll give you the synonyms of things that people are also actually searching for and then it'll also tell you your competition and stuff like that. So you'll be able to narrow down what your keyword phrases really are. And by the way, when I say keywords, I do mean keyword phrases. I don't just mean one specific word. It can be phrases, long-tail keyword phrases, and there's all different reasons to use them. But definitely use Google, Google's tool, it's a great tool. Google, Google's keyword tool. It's a tongue twister. Yeah, well that's Google. I also talk about Google University, because like it or not, whatever Google says is accurate. Yeah, that comes into play with building your digital DNA. But so the second part to that is your targeted metadata. Metadata is the code on, well, metadata is a piece of code on the back end of your website. Google doesn't see how pretty your website is, they see code. So metadata is kind of the key places that Google looks for keywords. As a very simple, simple example is up at the top. This is called a title tag, and it says home staging Orlando, or home staging Orlando company, professional staging Orlando, that's happened to be some of her keywords. And if you Google them, the keywords are there. So that's very, very simplistic, it's not that easy. But that's a pretty good example of metadata and how it can play in with your keywords. Number four is gonna be a blogging platform. We love WordPress. WordPress is my favorite. I put every single website we do onto WordPress. Usually the question that I get with WordPress is, well, isn't there a design limitation with WordPress because they're used to these templates, and sometimes they can be a bit challenging to update. And the answer is no. There's no design limitations whatsoever. I can overwrite anything in WordPress and make it do whatever I want it to do. So anybody that tells you any different either doesn't know what they're doing, or they're trying to get more money out of you. Because you can really make WordPress do whatever you want. The wonderful thing, the wonderful piece about WordPress and the reason we love it so much is for really for two reasons. One, it makes updating your website very, very simple. You know, there's always that, there's an app for that on your iPhone for whatever you possibly could ever need. With WordPress, there's a plugin for that. So you no longer have to know HTML code and have to code things. You can do chat boxes and videos and social media icons and social bookmarking icons and pop-ups and landing pages and all these things that you need as a marketer. You can now do with WordPress with just simple plugins. So that's one of the reasons it makes things updating. And two is really for the search engines. You know, I say Google, but when I say Google, I mean all of the search engines. But Google likes to see two things. One, they like clean code on the back end of your website so that they don't have to cut through the crap to get to the meat of your site. And WordPress is very, very clean versus having to hard code everything. And two, they really like what's called the hierarchy structure. So if you think of a Christmas tree, there's a top and it has branches, it has more branches and all that. That's what Google likes to see because it makes it very, very simple for them to spider and index the interior pages. So they come into your main site, they may go into your parent sites underneath that, and then they go into what's called the child pages underneath that. So they may come in through your main site, go to a blog page, and then under the blog category, there's whatever the blog is. And so there's all these different blogs where they come into the main page and they go to the about page. And then they find an about as a bio of every single one of the partners. So it makes it very, very easy for them to spider and index your sites. Number five is gonna be lead capture with autoresponders. This is one of the biggest mistakes that I see on people's websites is not having lead capture on your site. And that is the largest mistake you could possibly make because if you're spending any amount of money or time getting somebody to your website and your website's not doing its job to convert them, then you've just wasted that time and money because you have a split second for somebody to make up their mind. So if you're not capturing their information, they're probably never gonna come back to your website. And so first you have to capture them through lead capture. And then second you have to be able to follow up with them. And to follow up with those automated responses, those autoresponders, whether you can do it just through email, you can do broadcasts, so it's mass emails, but somehow have a follow up campaign for every single sign up on your website. One of the things that I like, I display on our site is that I actually tested this sign up, this sign up, which you'll notice are the same. This is a scroll that scrolls through, so it has a couple different offers. Sometimes it just has headlines basically just like does your website suck? And then there's this one. We actually tested this on all three places. This was our number one converter and it is our number one converter on every single site that we put it on. It is a WordPress plugin called Opting Crusher. And I've studied heat maps, I've studied click-throughs for whatever reason. Maybe it's because this doesn't actually leave unless you X out of it, so even if you scroll down, this stays there. So it's kind of always there, I guess. It's the number one on every single website we put it on. The other thing, yeah, go ahead. So also, what about the pop-up card? Have you tested those as well? Are they really effective? I know probably a few people were hemming before you even see everything else in the pop-ups. Pop-ups, so the answer is always gonna be have to test. Because no matter what market you're in, it's always gonna be different. I can say that from what I've seen, like one-click upsells, those types of pop-ups work really, really well. Doing pop-ups before you've established any type of relationship become annoying. So always test because that's not a hard-pressed rule, but it's a pretty good one. I like this one because if they want, they can close out of it, but it's not obtrusive enough that somebody immediately comes to the website and close out of it immediately, that type of thing. The other thing with lead capture is make sure that it's on every single page of your website. Make sure you have some sort of lead capture on every single page of your website. That's why we typically use that sidebar as kind of that information. Because if you only have it on your resources page, or only have it on your homepage, then you're making the assumption that somebody is going to remember to get back to that homepage, or remember to get back to that resources page to sign up for that special report that they kinda saw when they were scrolling. It's not gonna happen. So make sure that you have that information so that they're ready, they raise their hand and say, hey, I'm ready to give you my information, but you're there, and they can sign up for your stuff. So normally the questions I get on what do I kind of suggest for some sort of CRM system, and there's three that I suggest, depending on your bearing level, as well as your commitment income, or what's the word I'm looking for, to spend on a monthly basis, that's what I'm looking for. The first one's always gonna be Infusionsoft. It is the most robust, it is the best one I believe out there. That is what we personally use as a company. One of the things I like about Infusionsoft is it allows me to have lots of different companies under one location. So that was a problem before we found Infusionsoft. We had another CRM, and so we had one for celebrity sites. We had one for celebrity press publishing, we had one for celebrity films, we had one for Dixie and Agency, on and on and on. So not only did I have lots of different logins, but I had to remember that I had leads in different places. So Infusionsoft allowed me to get everything all in one place. It also allows me to do much more robust things than just a form code with autoresponders. So that's the most robust, it's also the most expensive. The second one down from that that I would suggest is one shopping cart. One shopping cart allows you to have form codes and autoresponders as well as a shopping cart system attached to it. So if you want to do a book or a DVD series or vitamins or whatever it is, it allows you to have that shopping cart capability on your website. Infusionsoft also has that as well. Aweber is gonna be the third one that I suggest. And Aweber is just the most simplistic, it is also the cheapest. It is just form codes and autoresponders, that's it. So there's no shopping cart, there's no flashy stuff. The problem that I find with one shopping cart and Aweber is you are, if you have a business that has different facets and you need to be able to market from the different companies, you have to have multiple different CRMs. You have to have multiple one shopping cart accounts, multiple Aweber accounts. But if you are just looking for that to start off, it's great to start off with. Did you have a question? Okay, so lead capture with autoresponders. Number six is gonna be CTAs or calls to action. Calls to action is probably the second biggest mistake that I see on people's websites. Very rarely do I see websites that are lacking in content. A lot of my clients have wonderful content, but they get what I call analysis paralysis. So they come to your website and they have no idea where to go. And you may know where to go, but it's also your baby, it's also your business. So it might make sense to you, but if somebody is coming to your website and just don't know where to go, then you're gonna immediately lose them. Because remember, it's like that, that people have to make a judgment on whether they want to stay on your page or not. So make sure you have calls to action. This is just a very simple example of one. This is John, it says find a chapter of coaching services and real life skills university. Those are the three main areas of his business. So those are the three main calls to action. But don't just think about calls to action on your homepage. You should have calls to action everywhere. So if you're on your services page and they're reading your services and they like your services, don't assume that they're then gonna click on the contact button to go there. Put at the bottom, you know, ready for a free consultation, ready for something, click here now. So give them something to do. Number seven is gonna be social media links. Social media, it's the buzzword today, we all know that. But the misconception about social media is that you can send out a tweet and you're gonna get immediate ROI back. And in some cases you might do that. There are some wonderful info marketers out there that will have a product, do a product launch, they send tweets out and they immediately get things back. That's great. But I would say that most other businesses, financial planners, that sort of thing, they're not just gonna send a tweet out about, it's tax season and immediately get something back. That's not how it works. But what social media does is social media is a wonderful third party verificator of who you are and what you do, that you are the go-to expert in that industry. People want to connect on social media. It's kind of one of those things that, well, everybody's on social media. You know, every corporation, every business is on social media, so if you're not, why not? Because everybody else is. So if people want to connect with you on social media to show that you're an expert, we like having the social media links right out in front, whether it's in the header or the sideboard, but somewhere that they can connect with you if that's how they want to connect with you. And that's super important to allow people to connect with you how they want to connect with you. So whether that's on your blog or whether that's picking up the phone and calling you, whether that's signing up for a mailing list, whether that's connecting through social media, people connect in different ways, so give them those options. One of the other things about social media is that it's indexed by search engines. So, you know, your Facebook, although your Facebook status updates won't all be indexed, your Facebook profile is. So that's part of your digital DNA and that's just another spot in Google that can take up when somebody Googles you, oh, Lindsay Dix's Facebook account. Okay, let me go click here and go check it out. So make sure it's out in front. Number eight is gonna be RSS feeds and social bookmarking icons. RSS stands for really simple syndication. It is kind of those little orange buttons that you typically see either in your browser, the bar at the top, or on specific blogs, articles, whatever it is. It allows somebody to get fed that information immediately into their inbox. So most of the time people ask me, well, you know, don't you want them to sign up for something and actually get their name and email address? And my answer is yes, absolutely. That's what you ultimately want them to do. I mean, really I want them to call and just hand me their credit card in truth. But if somebody doesn't want to sign up, if somebody doesn't want to call you and give you your credit card, if they don't want to sign up on your mailing list, you know, they're very skeptical, then they can sign up through an RSS feed and get the information on their terms. And you're still staying in the forefront of your mind because every time that you add a blog or article or whatever it is that's getting fed into their inbox, so when they're ready, when they're ready, they'll pick up the phone and call you. So make sure you have the RSS feeds. Social Bookmarking icons, it's second to that. Does everybody know what social bookmarking is? Okay, so social bookmarking, you know when you are browsing around in your computer and you want to get back to something, you bookmark it in your computer. That is a personal bookmark. Social bookmarking is that concept only at social. So it allows you to bookmark your content in these different websites and you can bookmark it with keywords. So people searching for that type of information comes across your information and they see it and then hopefully go back to your website. So social bookmarking allows people to share your content online. You can also share your own content online, which is part of our strategy. But if again, if people want to share that and that's something that they're really active in doing, make sure that it's there so that it's easy for them to share. It's also kind of one of the things about having social media links is, you know, if they want to post your stuff on social media and make it easy for them to do. So social bookmarking icons. Number nine is going to be frequently updated content. There's two reasons that I always suggest frequently updated content. The one is a very simple reason of when somebody comes to your website and they see that your blog hasn't been updated in 10 months and it's pretty outdated. The first thing that they're going to think is you too are also outdated because you have not updated your blog. So make sure that things are current on your website and you are constantly updating your content on your site. It also gives Google a reason to come back to your website. If they see a static website with no content being updated, they're not going to come to your website and they're not going to continue to spider it. So if you keep feeding them more current relevant content, they're not going to continually come back to your site. Content is king on the internet. It always will be. At the end of the day, that is what SEO is. It's about good keywords, links and content. I mean, that's all it is. Make sure you have that frequently updated content so that I can better explain the digital DNA and a little bit. We use blogs, articles and press releases and we separate those out. Those are kind of the three sections on our websites that you'll see most often. This is my personal website, so I kind of changed it up. And this is my SEO and SSM alert social media marketing that I do every Friday. And then my latest articles as well. Every Friday when I remember. 10 is going to be testimonials. Testimonials, it's just third party credibility of who you are and what you do. I can stand up here all day long and say how awesome I am. But if Kevin back there gets up and says that I'm awesome, that means a little bit more. Because it's a third party credibility of who you are and what you do. So testimonials are so important on your website. Text is wonderful. If you can get a photo, photo is obviously better. If you can get video testimonials, very even better. The other thing that you can do about testimonials is don't just think about a testimonials page or a success stories page or whatever, reviews page or whatever you want to call it. Think about key areas within your website that you can put a testimonial. So if somebody is going to look at a product, is there some sort of rollover or pop-up that you can do of a testimonial for that specific product that helps them in that sale? On a very simplified example of that is this. So this is our website. So when you roll over and go to type in your name and email address, a little pop-up window kind of looks like a sticky pad pops up. And it's from Dan Janal of PR Leeds, who is a pretty prominent person in my industry. And it says, thanks for publishing this every week. I always find articles useful. And you save me a lot of time from having to describe to so many other sources. So that's a pretty good testimonial of somebody is going to sign up for my list, that from a guy that is saying, basically, I'm not going to spam you, that you're going to get great content. And when we put that on, we saw the opt-in rate go up. So think about the places on your website that you can include those testimonials, not just on a testimonials page. I know sometimes with my financial advisors, do we have any compliance guys in here? No? OK. So sometimes with them, they have problems with testimonials. But there's also lots of ways to get around that as well. If you are a best-selling author, you can do testimonials about your book. It's not about your service. It's about your book. So there's some things creative that you can do there too. So those are my 10 things that you have to have to have a website for what I call a marketing website. The second thing we're going to talk about is building your digital DNA. What's the first thing anyone does when they want to learn more about you? They Google you. And like it or not, like I said earlier, Google University is accurate. So there's two ways to search. There is one, what we call a keyword-based search. So that's when somebody searches a relay in a social media agency, and you hope that your website comes up first. They are looking for a problem, and you need to be the solution. But what I'm going to talk about is what we call the referral search. So when somebody has gotten your business card, they heard you speak. They heard you on a live stream, on a webinar, on a tele-seminar. They got a piece of mail from you. Somehow they got a hold of your name, and they got a hold of your company, and they want to figure out if you are legitimate or not. They go to Google, and they check you out. This is what we call your digital DNA. For example, if you Google Lindsey Dix, I dominate the first four pages of Google. I can promise you that if I can do it for my last name, so can you. Because if you wait, yep, there it is. My last name was not an easy thing to overcome. There was a lot of Lindsay Lohan things when I first started this, which is one of the reasons that I did not change my last name professionally because it is Spanish. And I had already built a business around this, so I wasn't going to try to redo everything. But this is what we call your digital DNA. So when you Google me, yes, my websites come up, but my blogs come up, my articles come up, my press releases come up, my videos come up, my photos come up on and on and on and on. So this is what you want. It is based on a very fundamental SEO strategy, and SEO gets a lot more complicated than this. But this is pretty much the basics of it. Red in the center, that is your money site. That is the hub of everything, that is your main website. Around that, you have your content sites. So those are your article sites, your press release sites, your blog sites. Those are content sites outside of your main site. The blue ones are going to be your social media sites. What Google likes to see is a link to a link to a link to a link, linking all that, because you can pass link choose from one website to the other. So this is a very fundamental SEO strategy that uses social media, content marketing, all generating links back to your site. So, I think this is yeah, mainly the same thing. So let's talk about how we do that. The first one is going to be with blogs. So the strategy that we use in-house is we take your blog, we post it to your website, we then use social bookmarking and bookmark your site in all of the social bookmarking sites. And then we take one of those links, so call it dig. There's pretty much five main social bookmarking sites, dig Delicious Technoradi, StumbleUpon, and Reddit. Those are pretty much five big ones, but if you go to this website, socialpostor.com, which is an aggregated site of all the social bookmarking sites, there's hundreds of them. And so please not best use of your time is to create logins for all those higher temp or go to Fiverr or something and get somebody to create logins. But once you have logins to all of these accounts, you simply put in the URL of your blog, not your main website, the actual blog itself, your title, your text, your tags, which are your keywords and hit submit, and it's social bookmarks to all of these different websites. We take one of those links. So say it's the dig link that you just dug that blog in. And we post it on Facebook, Twitter, LinkedIn, and Google Plus because that innately takes that link to a link back to your website. And it kind of, so on Facebook, if you have a Facebook link, it takes it to the dig and then hopefully dig, make sure you have a link at the bottom. And that will then link back to your website. Link to a link to a link. Does that make sense? So. So. So. So. Do you have a specific question? Okay. So the second part to what we do is articles. So we use sites like Tumblr. That's what Tumblr looks like. And then we also use squadoo. There's a whole bunch of them. And we do, we alternate, alternate basically between the different article sites that we actually post the content on. The reason we do that is it looks more natural to Google. They like to see natural linking, natural content publishing. So we use a whole bunch of different article publication sites. So again, we take an article that you wrote, we post it to one of these sites. At the bottom there's always a byline that says, learn more about Lindsay Dixon, celebrity sites go here. That's the difference between social bookmarking and article syndication. So social bookmarking, when you bookmark it, it's your headline and it has like a paragraph of your content. So when you click on it, it takes you to your website. Article syndication is a completely separate content hub of your specific content. So we take that article and we post it on either squadoo, Tumblr, there's other ones that we use, and then we take that link and push that link out through social media, Twitter, Facebook, LinkedIn, Google Plus. So again, it creates that link to a link, to a link back to your website. Makes sense? The third, and so this is why obviously if you're following this, this is why we do blogs, articles, and press releases on people's websites. The third one's going to be press releases, as I just said, there's two sources that we use. The first one is prlog.org. The second one is gonna be pitch engine. PRlog.org is free. Pitch engine I think is $39 a month, something like that, but it's very cost effective. We don't use PR web. I find better results on these and it's cheaper. So prlog.org. I think they also have .com now, and then pitch engine. So again, with the press releases, we'll take a press release, we will write it and put it on, actually we'll post it to your website, write it and put it on here, and then we'll take that link from either PRlog or pitch engine and push it out through social media. Again, it's linking back to the press release, that links back to your website. So same concept, just with three different medias. Yeah. How is this different than using Tumblr or Squidoo? So Tumblr or Squidoo, I mean technically you could publish a press release on there, but these are actual press release sites. So everything you see on here is a press release of some sort. So what we take a strategy for press releases, most of the time when you write a press release, it has to be pretty earth shattering to get picked up. I mean you have to be really newsworthy. Online press releases are totally different. If you have your keywords in the correct places, your website, your press release will get picked up. So we actually write press releases on that you just published a blog or an article. So what we do is we take a blog and do three steps to increase your Facebook followers. We then switch it up and do six steps to better your Facebook campaign. It's the exact same thing, we just changed the headline and added three more tips, but we changed it just enough for search engines without reinventing the wheel. And then we take press releases and write, social media expert Lindsey Dix publishes new blog, three tips to increasing your Facebook followers. Social media expert Lindsey Dix publishes new article, six steps to whatever I said that article title was. And then we push those out through these sources. So don't think about you having to recreate the wheel and recreate all of this content. You take one very simple piece of an idea and you can create a blog off of it, an article off of it, two press releases off each one of those and you very quickly have four pieces of content, written content that you can push it out. Does that help answer your question? Okay, so taking that one step further because there's kind of five different ways that we do content online. Blogs, articles, press releases, video is gonna be the fourth one. Two of the video sites that I recommend, one is EZWebVideo and I don't remember how much it is, oh you can try, this is maybe a little bit old, but it is a paid service, but this is a great website for two reasons. One, you can add calls to action within your video so at the two minute mark something will pop up and tell them to do something so you can edit your videos. Two, it also is an aggregated site to be able to submit your videos to all of the video sites. So most people think YouTube, YouTube is not the only video site out there, there is a lot more than just YouTube. So there's Daily Motion, there's Bliv, there's Vimeo, there's a whole bunch of them. So every time you syndicate something online, if it's through YouTube, you should be doing it through all of them. For example, right now if you were to Google me, there's a video for me that's popping up from Daily Motion. Well that exact same video is also on YouTube, but Google from the links or whatever, the shares or whatever that particular video is getting, that's the authoritative video in Google's eyes, so they're ranking that one right now. So EZWebVideo, if you don't want to pay and you don't want to edit your videos, the second site that does that syndication portion of things is OneLoad. It used to be TubeMogul and then it changed to OneLoad. They now have two services, it paid one and a free one, this is the free one. So that's video. So if you don't have, I mean obviously you should be doing it for things like this and stuff, be pushing that video out there, but if you don't have video content, you can very easily take your article and put some slides to it, make a Camtasia video and now you have a video. So again, thinking on that one piece of, that one idea you have, you have a blog, you have an article, you have two press releases, you now have a video, you can write a press release that you just published a video, so now you have six pieces of content or whatever that was. Taking that a step further, that video that you just created, you can now strip the audio from that video and you have a podcast that you can now share online. So this is Podbean, I believe it's $97 a year and then there's also Podcast Alley. We have no affiliation with this, but it's just, it's a great little niche podcast site. Literally you can drill, like this one says chiropractic online something, I mean you can really drill down to the specific niche industries on this particular site, so that's kinda cool. So if you think again about that one idea, sorry, if you think that one idea, you have a blog, an article, two press releases off of each one of those, a video with a press release, and an audio with a press release, very quickly you have lots and lots of content that you can be distributing online and remember content is king. That is how you get your digital DNA to look like mine and get that third party credibility and the great thing about all that is it's stuff that you're putting out there. It's stuff that you want people to see, so you want them to see your blogs and articles because every single one of those is solidifying in your head that you're that expert, ultimately building that living legend status. Does that make sense? Okay, a couple other resources that I have really, really quickly that I find pretty cool. First one is Hootsuite. Hootsuite allows you to post to Twitter, Facebook, LinkedIn, and Google Plus all at the same time. So one of the questions that I get sometimes is, you know, how do you have time to log into Facebook and log into LinkedIn and do all this sort of stuff? Well, one, I have a team that does it now, but two, we don't recreate the wheel. There's a wonderful website out there that you can just post it once and it automatically posts to all of your accounts, simplifying life. The second one is going to be iftt.com. It stands for if that, then this. So you can create up rules within this website to do things based on other actions. So if I post a blog, then social book market. If I social book market, then post it to Twitter, Facebook, LinkedIn, Google Plus. You can create that string of rules to do things one right after the other. Questions?
